name: Billy Byers
abstract: William Mitchell Byers (May 1, 1927 – May 1, 1996) was an American jazz trombonist and arranger.

about_quotes.speaker: Quincy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I had all my people on the train – slow train to Yugoslavia – and Billy Byers. We went to Paris and knocked that sucker out in two days and got to Yugoslavia about the same time as the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Quincy Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: Some of the successful writers for the band were Benny Carter and Quincy Jones and Billy Byers. Those were three masters, all of whom contributed some very significant music to the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 7, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Manny Albam
about_quotes.evidence: There was a time in the 50s that we had an arranging office. And in the office was Billy Byers, Bob Brookmeyer, Al Cohn and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Manny Albam interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: The name 'Billy Byers' appears only in the [INTERVIEWER]'s turn listing credits for a music score and in a garbled list of personnel. The [INTERVIEWEE] does not mention Billy Byers, nor does he respond to the interviewer's question about Billy Byers with any personal anecdote or confirmation of a re

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: There was a show called “Free and Easy.” ... score by Quincy Jones and Billy Byers. ... I went to Europe with the Quincy Jones band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: I had met him when I was playing there with Buddy Rich and we hung, Billy and I started hanging a lot when I was getting my card.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
